topic: My house is currently listed for sale on Zillow with twelve photographs that show furniture from three apartments I lived in years ago and the listing agent's office burned down in two thousand three
title: My House Is Listed for Sale on Zillow. I Did Not List It. The Photos Are from My Old Apartments. | True Scary Story
description_intro: I was scrolling Zillow on a Saturday morning and an alert showed me a new listing on my own street. The address was my house. The exterior photo was my house. The interior photos were not. They showed rooms staged with furniture I had owned in three different apartments over the previous ten years. The listing agent's name belonged to a man who died in two thousand four. His office had burned down in two thousand three.
description:
I was scrolling Zillow on a Saturday morning and an alert showed me a new listing on my own street. The address was my house. The exterior photo was my house. The interior photos were not. They showed rooms staged with furniture I had owned in three different apartments over the previous ten years. The listing agent's name belonged to a man who died in two thousand four. His office had burned down in two thousand three.

The asking price was forty thousand dollars below market. The listing showed an open house scheduled for the following Saturday at two in the afternoon. I drove past my own street that Saturday at two and there was a small group of strangers walking up my driveway. A woman in a navy blazer was holding the door open for them. The door was unlocked. I was sitting in my driveway in my own car.

There is a detail in the listing's interior photographs that I have only shown my sister. The photograph of the master bedroom shows a person sleeping in the bed. The person is me. Watch to the end. The listing is still active. The price is dropping every Sunday.

#scarystories #truescarystories #horrornarration #analoghorror #scarystoriestotellinthedark #truehorrorstories #creepypasta #foundtapes
tags: scary stories to tell in the dark

sections.narration: I was on Zillow at the kitchen table on a Saturday morning, scrolling through listings the way I do most weekends. A push notification on my phone told me a new house had been listed in my saved search. The price had triggered an alert. The address was on my own street. I tapped through. The header photograph was the front of my house. Same color shutters. Same crooked downspout. Same row of hostas along the foundation. The asking price was forty thousand dollars below my last appraisal. The listing showed twelve interior photographs. The first one was my kitchen. The countertops were granite. My countertops are laminate. I have lived in this house for six years. I have not put my house on the market.

close detail of a phone screen showing a push notification from a real estate app, the screen glow lighting a wooden table, horizontal 16:9 framing

close detail of a granite kitchen countertop with stainless appliances behind it, no figures visible, horizontal 16:9 framing

sections.narration: The house is on Sumter Street in a small subdivision built in the late nineteen seventies. There are sixty houses on the street. Mine is the only two story colonial. The neighborhood was quiet enough that I knew the cars in every driveway and the dogs on every leash. I had bought the house from an estate sale at the end of two thousand nineteen. The previous owner had been a retired civil engineer named Walter. His widow had moved to Florida the year before. Property records showed three previous owners going back to the nineteen seventies. The most recent online listing for the address had been mine. There had been no listings since I bought the house. There were no for sale signs anywhere on the street.
image_prompts: wide exterior of a quiet suburban street at golden hour with rows of late nineteen seventies single family houses, no people or moving cars, horizontal 16:9 framing
close shot of a small green street sign reading Sumter Street mounted on a corner post, a power line crossing behind it, horizontal 16:9 framing
wide shot of a single two story colonial house among ranches, the only one of its design on the block, dim afternoon light, horizontal 16:9 framing
close detail of a county property records page printed on plain paper, three previous owner names listed in a column, horizontal 16:9 framing
wide exterior of an empty suburban driveway with a closed garage door and a single porch light just turned on, no figures, horizontal 16:9 framing

sections.narration: I have browsed Zillow on weekends for as long as I have owned the house. It is a habit my wife teases me about. She calls it window shopping for places we are never going to move to. I keep three saved searches. One for our zip code. One for the lake town an hour north where her parents had a cabin. One for the part of Sumter Street where my brother used to live before he relocated. The lake town saved search rarely turns up anything. The zip code saved search turns up maybe one new listing a month. The Sumter Street saved search had not produced an alert in two and a half years. My address had been the most recent listing before that.

wide interior of a sunlit kitchen with a laptop open on a kitchen island, a mug of coffee beside it, no people, horizontal 16:9 framing

close shot of a laptop screen displaying a saved searches column with three items listed, the cursor hovering on the third, horizontal 16:9 framing

sections.narration: I scrolled through the twelve interior photographs slowly. The kitchen had granite I did not own. The dining room had a heavy oak table that I recognized from the first apartment my wife and I had rented in two thousand fourteen. We had left the table in the apartment when we moved out. The living room held a brown leather sectional that I had owned alone in graduate school, in two thousand ten, in a one bedroom in a different city. The master bedroom had a four poster bed I had never seen before in my life. The bathroom showed a clawfoot tub that had been in the second apartment we rented, the one in Athens, before our daughter was born. The closet in the photograph held three of my own work shirts.
image_prompts: wide interior of a staged dining room with a heavy oak table and four matching chairs, soft afternoon light through a window, no people, horizontal 16:9 framing
close shot of a brown leather sectional sofa in a small living room with a rug and a single floor lamp, no figures, horizontal 16:9 framing

close shot of three folded work shirts on a wire hanger in an otherwise empty closet, the closet door slightly open, horizontal 16:9 framing

sections.narration: My first thought was that the listing agent had uploaded stock staging photos by mistake. Real estate agents do this sometimes when a home is empty. They paste in interiors from other listings to make the home feel furnished. My second thought was that someone had scraped photos off my old social media accounts. I had posted pictures of the leather sectional once, years ago, on a moving day post. My third thought was that this was a phishing scam. Some kind of identity theft tied to my address. I closed the browser tab. I opened it again. The listing was still there. I refreshed three times. The photographs were still the same. I copied the listing URL and emailed it to my wife at work with the subject line look at this.
image_prompts: wide overhead shot of a kitchen table with a laptop open and a smartphone beside it, the laptop screen glowing softly, no people, horizontal 16:9 framing

close shot of a browser refresh button being clicked, the cursor frozen mid click, horizontal 16:9 framing

sections.narration: I scrolled to the bottom of the listing where the agent's contact information sat. The agent's name was Roger Falke. The brokerage was called Falke Realty. The phone number listed had a local area code. I called the number from the kitchen. The line rang twelve times and then disconnected. I searched the agent's name. The county newspaper archive had an obituary for Roger Falke dated November of two thousand four. He had owned Falke Realty until his death. The brokerage office had burned down in a fire in October of two thousand three. The fire had killed two clients who had been inside reviewing paperwork. The business had operated out of a rented storefront on Main Street that was now a tax preparation office. I drove there on Sunday morning.
image_prompts: close shot of a corded landline phone resting on a kitchen counter, the receiver lifted and held loosely, horizontal 16:9 framing
close detail of a printed obituary page from a small town newspaper with one name circled in red pen, horizontal 16:9 framing
wide exterior of a small Main Street storefront with a faded tax preparation sign in the window, no people, horizontal 16:9 framing
close detail of a scorched brick wall with charred wooden trim along the top, dim afternoon light, horizontal 16:9 framing
close shot of a laminated business card lying on a kitchen counter with the name Roger Falke barely legible, horizontal 16:9 framing

sections.narration: The tax preparation storefront was closed on Sunday but the woman who owned it lived in the apartment above. She came down when I rang the bell. She had owned the building since two thousand seven. She remembered the fire. The previous occupant had been Falke Realty. The fire had started in the rear office. There had been no business in the storefront for three years after the fire because the insurance investigation had dragged. The county records showed Roger Falke's real estate license had been deactivated the year after his death. I asked her if anyone had been in the building recently looking for the old business. She told me a man had come by the previous Tuesday and asked about renting the basement. He had paid for the keys in cash.
image_prompts: wide exterior of a small Main Street storefront on a Sunday morning with the closed sign hanging in the window, no people, horizontal 16:9 framing
close shot of a small apartment door above a storefront, a brass bell pull mounted beside the frame, horizontal 16:9 framing

sections.narration: The Zillow listing had an open house scheduled for the following Saturday at two in the afternoon. I drove past my own street that Saturday at one forty five. There were three unfamiliar cars parked along the curb in front of my house. A small group of strangers was walking up my driveway, two couples and an older woman with a notepad. A woman in a navy blazer was standing at my front door holding it open for them. The door was unlocked. My wife was at her sister's house for the weekend. I was supposed to be visiting my mother. I had told no one my house would be empty. I pulled to the side of the road three houses down and watched the strangers go inside. I sat in the car for almost an hour.
image_prompts: wide exterior of a suburban street at midafternoon with three sedans parked along the curb in front of a two story colonial house, no people in frame, horizontal 16:9 framing
close shot of a group of three figures walking up a residential driveway from behind, only their backs visible, soft afternoon light, horizontal 16:9 framing

close shot of an unlocked front door slightly ajar with a navy blazer sleeve visible at the edge of the frame, no face, horizontal 16:9 framing

sections.narration: After the last of the strangers had driven away, I waited fifteen more minutes and then walked up to my own front door. It was locked. I let myself in with my key. The house was empty. The granite countertops were gone. My laminate ones were back. The brown leather sectional was not there. My usual couch was where it had always been. The four poster bed was gone. Our queen mattress was on its standard frame. I checked every room. Nothing was missing. Nothing had been moved. I sat at the kitchen table and opened Zillow on my phone. The listing was still active. I scrolled to the master bedroom photograph and zoomed in. The four poster bed had a person sleeping under the covers. The face was turned toward the lens. It was my face.
image_prompts: wide interior of a familiar residential entry hall with afternoon light through a side window, no figures, horizontal 16:9 framing

sections.narration: I did not call my wife. I did not call the police. I told myself the photograph was a coincidence. I went to bed that night in the same queen bed in the same plain sheets. I checked Zillow the next morning. The listing was still active. The asking price had dropped by ten thousand dollars overnight. The price has dropped by ten thousand dollars every Sunday morning since. Last Sunday the asking price was below twenty thousand dollars. There is another open house scheduled for next Saturday at two in the afternoon. The agent on the listing is still Roger Falke. My wife has started waking up at two in the morning to find me sitting at the kitchen table with the laptop open. She asks me what I am doing. I tell her nothing.
